extracts.extract: Bring in the technologist early. Sometimes too early, but too early is better than too late.
The only way to avoid surprised disappointment is to communicate. Specs help: Will the table be sortable. If there's a dropdown, what are the values. Should the map zoom in all the way or not?
Use data to find out what the story is, but not (necessarily) to tell it. You have to put more than just a human face, you have to tell a whole story.
Humanizing data is a reporting challenge. Find the people whose numbers are being tracked. Get out from behind the desk. Track them down. Treat data stories like you treat every story.
Using data because it exists is like eating the cleanest food in the trash can. Understand: how it was collected, why it was collected, every column, purpose.
Automate everything you reasonably can. Make scripts idempotent. Never modify originals. Name things using least-to-most-specific pattern. Test to validate the original data.
extracts.chapter: Processes, Standards and Documentation for Data-Driven Projects

extracts.extract: Test to audit your transformations. Test when you assert novel facts. Write detailed logs. Document provenance of data. Use comments to explain the why.
extracts.chapter: Processes, Standards and Documentation for Data-Driven Projects

extracts.extract: To succeed when no one's in charge: Listen hard (no project vocab/shorthand). Respect: Focus. Respect: Park ego. Start project vocab early. Document early, often and in public. Signal all turns\!
extracts.chapter: Processes, Standards and Documentation for Data-Driven Projects

extracts.extract: Hoard data. Let yourself fall down the rabbit hole. Go to a federal agency and see what's available.

Make sure it's a story before you say it's a story. Batch tasks. Do small tasks to fill in time (file FOIA while waiting for callbacks). Keep lists of stories to do: one list of short-turnarounds, one list of bigger projects.
